docs.title: RobotCode2026Public Documentation
docs.text:
This site documents 6328's 2026 robot code for Darwin. It is written for students, mentors, and contributors who need to understand how the code is organized, how to run it safely, and how new features should fit into the existing architecture.

The repository uses WPILib command-based programming, AdvantageKit logging and replay, generated Choreo trajectories, Northstar vision, and a strict hardware abstraction pattern. The most important idea is that subsystem logic should be deterministic and replayable: hardware data enters through IO input objects, robot logic acts on those inputs, and outputs are applied after the scheduler runs.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
If you are new to the codebase, read these pages in order:

  1. 101 Beginner explains the robot lifecycle, command-based structure, and the first files to open.
  2. 102 Developer Workflow covers setup, formatting, build commands, simulation, replay, deploy, and common troubleshooting.
  3. 103 Controls & Operations summarizes the current controller bindings, override switches, autonomous selection, and driver-facing dashboard state.
  4. 201 Architecture & AdvantageKit explains the IO pattern and why replayability drives the shape of the code.
  5. 202 Subsystems Overview maps the major packages to robot mechanisms and shared helper layers.
  6. 203 Autonomous & Pathing explains Choreo/VTS trajectory generation, auto selection, and auto command composition.
  7. 204 Vision & Object Detection describes Northstar, AprilTag fusion, object detections, and camera configuration.
  8. 205 Simulation & Replay covers the difference between physics simulation, log replay, and desktop no-op execution.

docs.title: Learning Paths
docs.text:
Use the path that matches the work you are doing:

Role or task Recommended pages New software student 101 Beginner, 102 Developer Workflow, Glossary Driver or operator 103 Controls & Operations, 205 Simulation & Replay Adding a mechanism 201 Architecture & AdvantageKit, 202 Subsystems Overview, Add a Subsystem Working on auto 203 Autonomous & Pathing, 205 Simulation & Replay, 302 Logging & Dashboard Working on vision 204 Vision & Object Detection, Implement Vision, 402 Hardware Reference Notes Reviewing a PR 401 PR Review Guide, 104 Documentation Maintenance

docs.title: Current Implementation Status
docs.text: This public branch contains the complete architecture and many subsystem implementations, but some real hardware IO classes are intentionally not present or not wired yet. RobotContainer currently creates SIMBOT implementations for physics simulation and falls back to no-op IO implementations for replay or unimplemented robot hardware paths. When adding real hardware support, keep subsystem logic unchanged and add the hardware-specific work behind the appropriate IO interface.

docs.title: Repository Map
docs.text: Area Path Purpose Robot startup and lifecycle src/main/java/org/littletonrobotics/frc2026/Robot.java Starts AdvantageKit, runs the scheduler, logs metadata, handles robot modes. Robot composition src/main/java/org/littletonrobotics/frc2026/RobotContainer.java Creates subsystems, controllers, auto selector, defaults, button bindings, and dashboard outputs. Constants and robot selection src/main/java/org/littletonrobotics/frc2026/Constants.java Defines loop timing, robot type, mode selection, and deploy/PR safety checks. Subsystems src/main/java/org/littletonrobotics/frc2026/subsystems Drive, launcher, intake, hopper, kicker, LEDs, vision, and related IO layers. Commands src/main/java/org/littletonrobotics/frc2026/commands Driver commands, auto commands, trajectory following, and high-level mechanism sequences. Deploy assets src/main/deploy AprilTag layouts and generated VTS/Choreo trajectories deployed to the RoboRIO. Simulation GUI files sim WPILib simulation layout and keyboard mapping files. Northstar coprocessor code northstar Vision worker code and related coprocessor utilities. MkDocs site docs_site The documentation you are reading.

docs.title: Documentation Workflow
docs.text:
Docs are source files, not generated artifacts. Edit Markdown in docs_site, run a strict build, and let GitHub Pages publish from CI.

python3 -m pip install -r requirements-docs.txt
python3 -m mkdocs serve
python3 -m mkdocs build --strict

The generated site directory is local build output and should not be committed.

docs.title: Working Principles
docs.text:
• Keep hardware details inside IO implementations.
  • Keep subsystem logic deterministic and based on logged inputs.
  • Prefer commands for behavior sequencing and subsystems for mechanism state/control.
  • Use AdvantageScope and logs to debug behavior before changing constants blindly.
  • Treat simulation and replay as first-class workflows, not afterthoughts.
  • Update documentation when adding a subsystem, controller binding, autonomous routine, dashboard output, or required setup step.

docs.title: 101: Welcome to FRC Software
docs.text: This page introduces the structure of the 2026 robot code and gives new contributors enough context to open the project without getting lost. The code is Java, uses WPILib's command-based framework, and is organized around AdvantageKit replayability.

docs.title: The Big Picture
docs.text:
Robot code runs in a fixed loop. In this project the loop period is 0.02 seconds, so the main control loop runs about 50 times per second. Each loop:

  1. Reads hardware inputs through IO classes.
  2. Logs those inputs through AdvantageKit.
  3. Runs subsystem periodic methods and scheduled commands.
  4. Computes new goals or outputs.
  5. Applies outputs to hardware or simulation.

That order matters. A subsystem should make decisions from the latest input snapshot, not by reaching directly into a motor controller whenever it wants information.

docs.title: Command-Based Programming
docs.text:
WPILib command-based programming splits robot software into a few recurring pieces:

Piece What it means in this repo Subsystem A robot mechanism or stateful service such as Drive, Slamtake, Hopper, Kicker, Hood, Flywheel, TrashCompactor, Vision, or Leds. Command A behavior that uses one or more subsystems, such as joystick drive, launch alignment, indexing, homing, or following a trajectory. RobotContainer The composition root. It creates subsystems, creates controller objects, configures autonomous choices, binds buttons to commands, and sets default commands. Robot The lifecycle owner. It starts logging, runs the scheduler, handles mode transitions, and records global telemetry.

Subsystems own mechanism state. Commands ask subsystems to do things. Controllers and dashboard choices schedule commands.

docs.title: Startup Flow
docs.text:
The startup path is:

  1. Main.java starts WPILib's robot runtime.
  2. Robot.java constructs the LoggedRobot.
  3. Robot records build metadata and configures AdvantageKit data receivers.
  4. Robot creates RobotContainer.
  5. RobotContainer creates subsystems, controllers, auto routines, default commands, and button bindings.
  6. WPILib calls robotPeriodic() every loop, which runs the command scheduler and the project's after-scheduler hooks.

When debugging startup behavior, begin with Constants.java, then Robot.java, then RobotContainer.java.

docs.title: Robot Modes
docs.text:
Constants.getMode() determines how the code talks to the outside world:

Mode When it is used What happens REAL Running on the RoboRIO with DARWIN or ALPHABOT selected. Logs to WPILOG and publishes live NT4 telemetry. SIM Running on desktop with the SIMBOT robot selected. Uses physics simulation IO implementations and publishes NT4 telemetry. REPLAY Running on desktop with DARWIN or ALPHABOT selected. Reads an existing WPILOG and writes a replay output log.

For physics simulation, run with SIMBOT selected through the environment:

SIMBOT=1 ./gradlew simulateJava

Without SIMBOT, desktop execution is intended for replay, not full physics simulation.

docs.title: Subsystems and IO
docs.text:
Most mechanisms are split into:

  • A subsystem class, such as Drive.java or Flywheel.java.
  • An IO interface, such as ModuleIO.java or FlywheelIO.java.
  • One or more IO implementations, such as ModuleIOSim.java.

The subsystem contains robot logic. The IO implementation contains hardware or simulator details. If a subsystem imports vendor hardware classes directly, the code becomes harder to replay and test.

docs.title: A Minimal Command
docs.text:
A command should declare which subsystem it uses and should return quickly every loop. In this project many simple commands are created with Commands.run, runEnd, startEnd, or subsystem helper methods.

public static Command stopDrive(Drive drive) {
  return Commands.run(drive::stop, drive);
}

The final drive argument is the requirement. It tells WPILib that this command owns the drive subsystem while scheduled, so conflicting drive commands are interrupted correctly.

docs.title: What to Read First
docs.text:
Start with these files:

  • Constants.java: robot selection, loop timing, tuning flag, deploy checks.
  • Robot.java: logging setup, scheduler execution, mode transitions, global telemetry.
  • RobotContainer.java: subsystem creation, button bindings, auto setup.
  • subsystems/drive/Drive.java: representative subsystem structure.
  • subsystems/drive/ModuleIO.java: representative IO interface with logged inputs and output state.
  • commands/DriveCommands.java: examples of command factory methods.
  • commands/auto/AutoBuilder.java: examples of combining smaller commands into autonomous routines.

docs.title: 102: Developer Workflow
docs.text: This page covers the normal development loop: clone, build, format, simulate, replay, deploy, and troubleshoot. All commands should be run from the repository root.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Install the normal WPILib Java development environment for the current season, including VS Code and the WPILib extension. This repository uses:

  • Java 17.
  • GradleRIO through the included gradlew wrapper.
  • Spotless with Google Java Format.
  • AdvantageKit and AdvantageScope for logging, replay, and visualization.
  • Vendor dependencies in vendordeps.

After cloning, install the recommended VS Code extensions when prompted.

docs.title: First-Time Setup
docs.text:
1. Open the repository root in VS Code.
  2. Let the WPILib extension detect the Gradle project.
  3. Copy the JSON files from sim into the project root if you want the provided simulation GUI layout and keyboard mapping.
  4. Configure AdvantageScope's custom assets folder if you are using project-specific assets.
  5. Run a build before making changes:
./gradlew build

The build compiles the code, runs configured checks, applies formatting, and generates deploy artifacts.

docs.title: Daily Development Loop
docs.text:
Use this loop for most changes:

  1. Pull or update your branch.
  2. Make a small, focused change.
  3. Run formatting or build.
  4. Run simulation or replay if the behavior can be checked without the robot.
  5. Review logs in AdvantageScope when the change affects runtime behavior.
  6. Commit with a clear message.

Useful commands:

./gradlew spotlessApply
./gradlew build
./gradlew simulateJava
./gradlew deploy

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text:
Spotless formats Java, Gradle, JSON, Markdown, and .gitignore files. Java uses Google Java Format and the project license header.

./gradlew spotlessApply

compileJava depends on spotlessApply, so ./gradlew build will also format code. Be aware of this when reviewing diffs: formatting changes may appear alongside functional changes if the branch was not already formatted.

docs.title: Building
docs.text:
Run:

./gradlew build

Important build behavior:

  • compileJava runs Spotless.
  • compileJava finalizes with generateDriveTrajectories.
  • generateDriveTrajectories creates or updates VTS/Choreo deploy files from DriveTrajectories.
  • The deploy artifact includes files under src/main/deploy.
  • Tests use JUnit 5 when tests are present.

docs.title: Constants Checks
docs.text:
Constants.java contains safety checks used by Gradle tasks:

  • checkConstantsDeploy prevents deploying with RobotType.SIMBOT.
  • checkConstantsPullRequest fails if the default robot is not DARWIN or if tuningMode is enabled.

Before merging, the default constants should be:

private static final RobotType robot = RobotType.DARWIN;
public static final boolean tuningMode = false;

docs.title: Physics Simulation
docs.text:
Use SIMBOT for full physics simulation:

SIMBOT=1 ./gradlew simulateJava

In SIMBOT mode, RobotContainer creates simulated IO for drive, slamtake, hopper, kicker, LEDs, and fuel simulation. The simulator also sets the team number to 6328 and uses the WPILib Driver Station simulator.

The sim GUI is disabled by default in build.gradle to support command-line replay workflows. The Driver Station sim is still added. Copy the files from sim into the project root to use the provided GUI layout and keyboard mappings.

docs.title: Log Replay
docs.text:
On desktop without SIMBOT, DARWIN and ALPHABOT run in REPLAY mode. AdvantageKit asks for a log file, reads it through WPILOGReader, and writes a new log with _sim appended to the path.

Replay is useful when you want to test deterministic logic changes against real match data. Hardware IO should not run during replay; the logged inputs are the data source.

docs.title: Documentation Preview
docs.text:
The documentation source lives in docs_site and is built with MkDocs Material. Install pinned documentation dependencies when needed:

python3 -m pip install -r requirements-docs.txt

Preview locally:

python3 -m mkdocs serve

Run the same strict check used by CI:

python3 -m mkdocs build --strict

The generated site directory is build output and should not be committed. See 104 Documentation Maintenance for writing and review expectations.

docs.title: Deploying
docs.text:
Connect to the robot network or tether over USB, then run:

./gradlew deploy

Deploy copies the Java artifact and src/main/deploy files to the RoboRIO. checkConstantsDeploy runs before deploy, so deploying with SIMBOT selected fails by design.

docs.title: Event Branch Auto-Commits
docs.text:
The eventDeploy Gradle task runs before createVersionFile. If a deploy task is being run and the current Git branch starts with event, the task stages all changes and creates a timestamped commit before deployment.

This preserves the exact code deployed at an event. It also means a deploy from an event... branch can create a commit even if you did not explicitly run git commit.

docs.title: Common Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause What to check Desktop run asks for a replay log You are running DARWIN or ALPHABOT on desktop. Use SIMBOT=1 ./gradlew simulateJava for physics sim. Deploy fails with invalid robot selected Constants.robot is SIMBOT. Restore DARWIN or ALPHABOT before deploying. Pull request check fails constants validation tuningMode is true or robot type is not default. Restore merge-safe constants. Choreo trajectory not found File missing under src/main/deploy/vts or name mismatch in code. Run ./gradlew build and check DriveTrajectories path names. Controller inputs look wrong in sim Simulation GUI mappings were not copied. Copy sim/*.json to the repository root and review sim/Simulation Key Mapping.html. AdvantageScope data is missing Wrong mode or no receiver configured for your workflow. REAL logs WPILOG+NT4, SIM publishes NT4, REPLAY writes a replay log.

docs.title: 103: Controls & Operations
docs.text: This page summarizes the current operator interface and the robot behaviors wired in RobotContainer. Treat it as a software reference for drive practice, simulation, and code review. If controls change in configureButtonBindings(), update this page in the same pull request.

docs.title: Controller Ports
docs.text:
Device Port Code object Primary driver controller 0 RazerWolverineController primary Secondary operator controller 1 CommandXboxController secondary Override switches 5 OverrideSwitches overrides

The robot creates alerts for missing primary, secondary, and override controllers. Primary and secondary disconnects are warnings; override disconnect is informational.

docs.title: Driving
docs.text:
The default drive command is DriveCommands.joystickDrive. It combines primary and secondary joystick inputs:

  • Forward/backward: -leftY.
  • Left/right: -leftX.
  • Rotation: -rightX.
  • Driver and secondary values are added together before deadband and scaling.

By default the drive is field-relative. Override switch 1 changes to robot-relative drive.

The joystick processing applies a 0.1 deadband and squares magnitude for finer low-speed control. Linear output is scaled by DriveConstants.maxLinearSpeed; angular output is scaled by DriveConstants.maxAngularSpeed.

docs.title: Primary Controller Bindings
docs.text:
Input Behavior Left bumper Align while launching, spin flywheel to tracked target, move hood to tracked target, and feed through hopper/kicker when all launch tolerances are met. Right bumper Force launch when hub state allows it, bypassing normal tolerance checks. Secondary right bumper can also trigger this. B Outtake through hopper, kicker, and slamtake roller. Right claw Unjam by outtaking hopper and kicker. Lower right paddle Outpost preset for flywheel and hood. Left claw Passing preset for flywheel and hood. Lower left paddle Tower preset for flywheel and hood. Upper left paddle Trench preset for flywheel and hood. Upper right paddle Force trash compactor to max position while held. Right trigger Retract intake and run intake briefly. Secondary right trigger can also trigger this. Left trigger Run intake and deploy slamtake while held in teleop. Secondary left trigger can also trigger this. A Drive under tower with a locked tower-facing rotation. X Force trash compactor down while held.

The left-bumper launch flow also rumbles the secondary controller if the robot is ready to launch but the hub period is inactive and the robot is not in passing mode.

docs.title: Secondary Controller Bindings
docs.text: Input Behavior Start + Back Reset gyro heading while preserving current translation. A Systems-check preset at minimum hood angle. Y Systems-check preset at maximum hood angle. X Run hood zero, slamtake home, and trash compactor min home unless B is also held. X + B Home trash compactor to max. Double-press B Force hood zero, force slamtake max zero, and force trash compactor min zero. POV right Increase hood angle offset by 0.2 degrees, repeating while held. POV left Decrease hood angle offset by 0.2 degrees, repeating while held. POV up Force trash compactor to max while held. POV down Force trash compactor to min while held. Left bumper Test flywheel spin-up at 200 rad/s while held. Right bumper Force launch, same as primary right bumper. Left trigger Run intake. Right trigger Retract intake and run intake briefly.

docs.title: Override Switches
docs.text:
Switch Behavior Driver switch 1 Robot-relative drive override. Driver switch 2 Superstructure coast override when disabled. Multi-direction left Force hub shift result to lost auto. Multi-direction right Force hub shift result to won auto. Operator switch 0 Disable flywheel auto spin-up. Operator switch 1 Ignore hub state and allow external hub counter override behavior. Operator switch 2 Ignore tilt checks for launch tolerance.

Coast override is only enabled while disabled. It is cleared automatically when the robot is enabled.

docs.title: Automatic Mode Behavior
docs.text:
When teleop starts:

  • The slamtake deploys.
  • Hood, slamtake, and trash compactor home if they are not already zeroed.
  • Hub shift timing is initialized.

When autonomous starts:

  • Hub shift timing and hub counter are initialized.
  • Object detection fuel poses are initialized.
  • Hood, slamtake, and trash compactor are force-zeroed.
  • The slamtake deploys after a short delay.
  • Hood runs its autonomous command.
  • Intake and flywheel automation run after configured delays.
  • The trash compactor changes behavior based on neutral-zone trench bounds.

When disabled:

  • Hub shift timing is reset.
  • Coast override is cleared on the next enable edge.

docs.title: Dashboard Outputs
docs.text:
RobotContainer.updateDashboardOutputs() publishes key driver information:

Dashboard key Meaning Drive At Goal Whether drive yaw is within launch/pass tolerance. Drive Pitch & Roll OK Whether tilt is within tolerance for launching or passing. Match Time Current Driver Station match time. Shifts/Remaining Shift Time Remaining time in the current hub shift period. Shifts/Shift Active Whether the shifted hub period is active. Shifts/Game State Current shifted hub state. Shifts/Active First? Whether the alliance is active first. Hood Angle Offset Current hood offset shown with one decimal place.

The AprilTag layout chooser also creates an alert if a non-default layout is selected.

docs.title: 104: Documentation Maintenance
docs.text: This page explains how to keep the MkDocs site accurate as the robot code changes. Treat documentation like code: it should be reviewed, built, and updated with the behavior it describes.

docs.title: Source and Build Output
docs.text:
The documentation source lives in docs_site. MkDocs reads mkdocs.yml and writes generated HTML into site.

Do not commit site. It is generated output and is published by GitHub Actions.

python3 -m pip install -r requirements-docs.txt
python3 -m mkdocs serve
python3 -m mkdocs build --strict

Use mkdocs serve while editing and mkdocs build --strict before opening or merging a pull request.

docs.title: When to Update Docs
docs.text:
Update docs in the same pull request when a change affects:

  • First-time setup or required tools.
  • Gradle commands, build behavior, deploy behavior, or CI.
  • Controller bindings, override switches, dashboard keys, or operator alerts.
  • Subsystem behavior, public commands, goals, or safety assumptions.
  • Autonomous routine names, path generation, dashboard questions, or deploy assets.
  • Vision camera IDs, mounts, frame formats, filters, recording behavior, or Northstar topics.
  • Hardware IDs, ports, bus names, offsets, current limits, or device configuration assumptions.
  • Logging paths that someone would use in AdvantageScope.
  • Simulation or replay behavior.

Small internal refactors do not need a documentation change unless they alter how contributors should work with the code.

docs.title: Page Ownership
docs.text:
Use the existing page structure before adding a new page:

Change type Page to update New contributor setup 102-workflow.md Driver/operator behavior 103-controls.md Architecture or IO boundary changes 201-intermediate.md or 301-architecture.md Mechanism package behavior 202-subsystems.md Autonomous and trajectory changes 203-auto.md Vision or object detection changes 204-vision-object-detection.md Simulation or replay changes 205-simulation-replay.md Logging paths or dashboard values 302-logging-dashboard.md Review expectations 401-mentor-review.md Hardware IDs and ports 402-hardware-reference.md Repeated implementation patterns Cookbook pages Acronyms or project vocabulary glossary.md

Add a new page only when the topic is large enough that it would make an existing page hard to scan.

docs.title: Writing Style
docs.text:
Write for the person doing the work, not for someone already familiar with the code.

  • Start each page with what the reader should use it for.
  • Prefer concrete file names, commands, log keys, and dashboard keys.
  • Explain the operational consequence of a rule, not just the rule.
  • Keep code examples short enough to copy and adapt.
  • Call out safety-critical behavior directly.
  • Use tables for mappings such as controls, constants, dashboard keys, and file ownership.
  • Keep historical notes only when they prevent real confusion.

Avoid documenting every implementation detail. The docs should explain stable architecture, workflows, and operational knowledge that is easy to lose.

docs.title: Links and Navigation
docs.text:
Use relative links between documentation pages:

[Simulation & Replay](205-simulation-replay.md)

Use inline code formatting for repository paths, class names, commands, constants, log keys, and dashboard keys.

When adding a page:

  1. Create the Markdown file under docs_site.
  2. Add it to nav in mkdocs.yml.
  3. Link to it from a relevant existing page.
  4. Run python3 -m mkdocs build --strict.

docs.title: Diagrams
docs.text:
MkDocs is configured for Mermaid diagrams. Use diagrams when relationships are easier to understand visually, such as lifecycle flow, data flow, or ownership boundaries.

Keep diagrams simple. If the diagram needs many labels to be understandable, split it into smaller diagrams or use a table.

docs.title: Review Checklist
docs.text:
Before merging documentation changes:

  • The page is listed in mkdocs.yml if it should be user-visible.
  • Links are relative and pass mkdocs build --strict.
  • Commands are copied exactly from the real workflow.
  • Log keys and dashboard keys match the code.
  • Hardware or controls notes match the current robot branch.
  • Generated site output is not committed.
  • Screenshots or external links are necessary and stable.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
The check-mkdocs workflow builds docs on pull requests and pushes to main. The deploy-mkdocs workflow publishes the site to GitHub Pages from main.

Both workflows install the pinned dependencies from requirements-docs.txt, so local builds and CI should use the same MkDocs toolchain.

docs.title: 201: Architecture & AdvantageKit
docs.text: The project is built around AdvantageKit's logged-input model. Subsystems should be deterministic functions of logged inputs, command state, constants, and time. Hardware access is pushed to IO implementations so the same subsystem logic can run on the real robot, in physics simulation, and during log replay.

docs.title: Why This Architecture Exists
docs.text:
FRC robots are difficult to debug if hardware reads, control decisions, and motor writes are mixed together. AdvantageKit solves much of that problem by making inputs explicit and replayable. If the code follows the pattern, a real match log can be replayed on a laptop and the logic should make the same decisions it made on the robot.

The architecture has three rules:

  1. Inputs from hardware go into @AutoLog input classes.
  2. Subsystems read input objects and write output state.
  3. IO implementations apply the output state to real hardware or simulation after the scheduler.

docs.title: IO Pattern
docs.text:
An IO interface defines the boundary between robot logic and hardware. For example, ModuleIO contains:

  • ModuleIOInputs: logged values read from hardware or simulation.
  • ModuleIOOutputs: desired output mode and setpoints.
  • updateInputs(...): fills the input object.
  • applyOutputs(...): applies the latest desired outputs.
public interface ModuleIO {
  @AutoLog
  public static class ModuleIOInputs {
    public boolean driveConnected = false;
    public double drivePositionRads = 0.0;
    public double driveVelocityRadsPerSec = 0.0;
    public double driveAppliedVolts = 0.0;
    public boolean turnConnected = false;
    public Rotation2d turnPositionRads = Rotation2d.kZero;
  }

  public static class ModuleIOOutputs {
    public ModuleIOOutputMode mode = ModuleIOOutputMode.COAST;
    public double driveVelocityRadPerSec = 0.0;
    public double driveFeedforward = 0.0;
    public Rotation2d turnRotation = Rotation2d.kZero;
  }

  public default void updateInputs(ModuleIOInputs inputs) {}
  public default void applyOutputs(ModuleIOOutputs outputs) {}
}

AdvantageKit generates ModuleIOInputsAutoLogged from the @AutoLog class. The subsystem or helper class owns one generated instance and logs it each loop:

io.updateInputs(inputs);
Logger.processInputs("Drive/Module" + index, inputs);

docs.title: Inputs vs Outputs
docs.text:
Inputs are measured facts. Examples:

  • Encoder position and velocity.
  • Motor applied voltage.
  • Supply or torque current.
  • Temperature.
  • Connection status.
  • Camera frame data.
  • Sensor-derived positions.

Outputs are requests. Examples:

  • Closed-loop velocity setpoint.
  • Open-loop voltage.
  • Brake or coast mode.
  • Position setpoint.
  • Feedforward value.

Subsystem logic may use inputs to decide future outputs. It should not use outputs as if they were measured state.

docs.title: Periodic and After-Scheduler
docs.text:
Many subsystems extend FullSubsystem, which supports both normal periodic logic and an after-scheduler hook. The common pattern is:

  1. periodic() reads IO inputs, logs them, updates alerts, computes state, and records telemetry.
  2. WPILib's command scheduler runs commands.
  3. periodicAfterScheduler() applies outputs after commands have had a chance to modify goals.

The drive module helper is a clear example. Module.periodic() reads/logs inputs and checks alerts. Module.periodicAfterScheduler() calls io.applyOutputs(outputs).

docs.title: Robot Lifecycle and Logging
docs.text:
Robot.java is responsible for global AdvantageKit configuration:

Mode Data path REAL WPILOGWriter and NT4Publisher are added. SIM NT4Publisher is added. REPLAY WPILOGReader is set as replay source and a _sim WPILOGWriter is added.

Robot records metadata such as project name, build date, Git SHA, Git branch, dirty state, hostname, and platform. These fields are essential for understanding which code produced a log.

docs.title: Determinism in Replay
docs.text:
Replay is only useful when robot logic does not depend on live hardware calls. During replay:

  • No real motor controller should be needed.
  • Logged input data is the source of sensor truth.
  • The code should write output telemetry into a replay log.
  • Behavior changes can be tested against old logs.

Avoid hidden dependencies on wall-clock timing, random values, or hardware APIs in subsystem logic. If time is needed, make sure replay behavior is intentional.

docs.title: Tunable Numbers
docs.text:
LoggedTunableNumber is used throughout the code for gains, tolerances, voltages, currents, and timing. These values appear in logs and NetworkTables, making it possible to tune behavior without recompiling in some workflows.

Common examples:

  • DriveCommands/Launching/kP.
  • DriveTrajectory/LinearkP.
  • Flywheel/kP, kV, kA.
  • Hood/ToleranceDeg.
  • TrashCompactor/Profile/MaxVelocity.
  • ObjectDetection/FuelPersistanceTime.

When adding a tunable number, choose a stable, descriptive path and document what physical behavior the value affects.

docs.title: Alerts
docs.text:
Subsystems use WPILib Alert objects for hardware faults and operator-visible warnings. Hardware alerts are suppressed in SIM and during the first 30 seconds of non-sim runtime through Robot.showHardwareAlerts() to avoid startup noise.

Common alert patterns:

  • Debounce connection status before showing an error.
  • Include device role and index in the message.
  • Do not use alerts as a replacement for logged inputs; log the connection state too.

docs.title: Current Branch Reality
docs.text: This branch currently has simulation implementations for several mechanisms and no-op fallbacks for replay/unimplemented hardware paths. Some real hardware classes are not present. That is acceptable as long as new work preserves the IO boundary. When a real implementation is added, it should implement the existing IO interface rather than changing subsystem logic to import vendor APIs.

docs.title: 202: Subsystems Overview
docs.text: This page maps the subsystem packages to the behavior they own. It is a starting point for code navigation and review, not a replacement for reading the source.

docs.title: Subsystem Design Pattern
docs.text:
Most mechanisms follow this shape:

Layer Responsibility Public subsystem class Owns high-level state, commands, logged outputs, alerts, setpoints, and interaction with other robot state. IO interface Defines logged inputs and output requests. IO implementation Talks to real devices or physics simulation. Shared helper Encapsulates repeated logic, such as rollers or swerve modules.

Subsystems should not know whether they are running real hardware, simulation, or replay.

docs.title: Drive
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/drive

Main classes:

  • Drive: owns four modules, gyro inputs, kinematics, brake/coast behavior, odometry observations, and drive setpoints.
  • Module: helper for one swerve module.
  • ModuleIO: drive and turn motor input/output boundary.
  • ModuleIOSim: physics simulation for a module using DCMotorSim.
  • GyroIO: gyro input boundary.
  • DriveConstants: robot dimensions, module translations, speed limits, reductions, CAN IDs, encoder offsets, wheel radius, mass, and trajectory constraints.

Drive.periodic() reads gyro and module inputs, logs them, sends odometry observations to RobotState, updates gyro alerts, and handles disabled brake/coast behavior. Drive.runVelocity() converts chassis speeds to module states, desaturates them, logs setpoints, and passes optimized state requests to each module.

The module order is front-left, front-right, back-left, back-right.

docs.title: RobotState
docs.text:
Path: RobotState.java

RobotState is a singleton service rather than a normal motor subsystem. It owns:

  • Odometry pose.
  • Estimated pose after vision fusion.
  • Pose, rotation, and slamtake angle buffers.
  • Robot-relative and field-relative velocity helpers.
  • Vision observation fusion.
  • Intake pose and intake velocity helpers.

Drive adds odometry observations. Vision adds timestamped AprilTag observations. Slamtake adds slamtake angle observations when enabled and zeroed.

docs.title: Slamtake
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/slamtake

Slamtake combines a deployable arm (Slam) and an intake roller (RollerSystem). It owns two goal enums:

  • IntakeGoal: INTAKE, OUTTAKE, STOP.
  • SlamGoal: DEPLOY, RETRACT, RETRACT_SLOW, IDLE.

Important behavior:

  • Intake and outtake voltages are tunable.
  • Deploy and retract are debounced before state changes to DEPLOYED or RETRACTED.
  • Slow retract uses a slew-rate limiter.
  • The measured slamtake angle is recorded into RobotState for timestamped camera pose calculations.
  • The measured angle is visualized in DarwinMechanism3d.

docs.title: Hopper
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/hopper

Hopper wraps one RollerSystem. Its goals are:

  • LAUNCH.
  • OUTTAKE.
  • STOP.

It uses tunable voltages for launch and outtake. In SIMBOT mode it can log and update the simulated fuel count through RobotContainer.SimFuelCount.

docs.title: Kicker
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/kicker

Kicker wraps two rollers: front and back. It owns:

  • Separate PID and feedforward tunables for each roller.
  • Roller radius conversion from launch surface speed to angular velocity.
  • Goals LAUNCH, OUTTAKE, and STOP.
  • SIMBOT launch depletion based on SimFuelCount.launchBPS.

In periodicAfterScheduler(), it reads LaunchCalculator parameters and converts the target surface speed into front and back roller setpoints.

docs.title: Launcher: Flywheel, Hood, and Calculator
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/launcher

The launcher is split into several parts:

  • Flywheel: controls the high-energy wheel setpoint with current/power budgeting, feedforward, optional bang-bang behavior, and at-goal checks.
  • Hood: controls launcher angle, homing, force-zeroing, tracking target angle, and 3D visualization.
  • LaunchCalculator: computes launch parameters and presets from robot pose and target geometry.
  • LauncherConstants: launcher geometry used by drive and launch calculations.

Flywheel limits acceleration based on the configured supply current budget, battery voltage, motor model, efficiency, and flywheel moment of inertia. Hood clamps commands between minAngle and maxAngle after zeroing.

docs.title: TrashCompactor
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/trashcompactor

TrashCompactor controls a vertical compaction mechanism with a trapezoid profile. Its modes are:

  • PASSIVE_DOWN.
  • FORCE_MAX.
  • FORCE_MIN.
  • LAUNCHING.
  • IDLE.

It supports homing to min or max, force-zeroing, passive current control, profiled position control, and 3D visualization. Launching mode captures the current measured height plus a tunable offset when the mode is entered.

docs.title: RollerSystem
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/rollers

RollerSystem is a shared helper used by hopper, kicker, and slamtake. It handles:

  • Logging RollerSystemIOInputs.
  • Motor/follower connection alerts.
  • Battery current reporting.
  • Brake/coast behavior while disabled.
  • Open-loop voltage control.
  • Closed-loop velocity with feedforward.

Use this helper when adding another simple roller mechanism.

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/vision

Vision is a VirtualSubsystem, not a motor mechanism. It reads camera data from VisionIO implementations, logs AprilTag and object detection frames, filters bad observations, updates Northstar recording/throttle state, and forwards accepted pose observations to RobotState.

VisionIONorthstar is the NetworkTables implementation for Northstar. It publishes camera configuration and receives output queues for AprilTags and object detections.

docs.title: LEDs
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/leds

Leds is a VirtualSubsystem used for driver feedback. RobotContainer sets boolean state such as:

  • superstructureCoast.
  • autoWinnerNotSet.
  • inLaunchingTolerance.

LedsIOHAL is the HAL-backed IO implementation. A no-op LedsIO is used when LEDs are not wired through a real implementation.

docs.title: HubCounter and Hub Shift
docs.text:
Path: subsystems/hubcounter, util/HubShiftUtil.java

HubCounter and HubShiftUtil track game-specific hub shift state and override behavior. RobotContainer initializes hub shift state on mode transitions, publishes dashboard outputs, rumbles controllers for missing game data, and lets override switches force the alliance win/loss state when needed.

docs.title: 203: Autonomous & Pathing
docs.text: Autonomous code combines generated Choreo/VTS trajectories, pose-based drive commands, mechanism commands, and dashboard-selected routine questions. The goal is to make autonomous routines configurable without scattering path names and decision logic throughout the project.

docs.title: Important Files
docs.text: File Purpose RobotContainer.java Creates the AutoSelector, registers routines, and returns the selected command to Robot. AutoSelector.java Publishes routine choices and up to six routine-specific questions to the dashboard. commands/auto/AutoBuilder.java Builds named autonomous routines from smaller commands and selected responses. commands/auto/AutoCommands.java Shared auto command helpers: trajectory following, drive-to-pose, indexing, launch helpers, bounds helpers. commands/DriveTrajectory.java Follows Choreo SwerveSample trajectories with PID feedback and optional omega override. subsystems/drive/DriveTrajectories.java Defines VTS path requests used by trajectory generation. util/vts/GenerateTrajectories.java Generates/updates the VTS Choreo project and .traj files. src/main/deploy/vts Generated .chor and .traj files deployed to the robot.

docs.title: Auto Selection Flow
docs.text:
RobotContainer.configureAutos() creates an AutoBuilder and registers routines with AutoSelector.

Currently registered routines include:

  • Monopoly Salesman.
  • Timid Salesman.
  • Combobulated Salesman for drive feedforward characterization.
  • Discombobulated Salesman for wheel radius characterization.

AutoSelector publishes a routine chooser and question choosers under SmartDashboard keys. Each routine can define up to six questions. Responses are stored as AutoQuestionResponse values and read by AutoBuilder suppliers when the command runs.

Robot.autonomousInit() calls robotContainer.getAutonomousCommand() and schedules the selected command.

docs.title: Choreo/VTS Directory
docs.text:
This project sets Choreo's deploy directory to:

src/main/deploy/vts

The setup is done reflectively in RobotContainer:

Choreo.setChoreoDir(new File(Filesystem.getDeployDirectory(), "vts"));

Use the path names exactly as referenced in code. AutoCommands.followTrajectory("DriveForward1m", ...) expects DriveForward1m.traj to be available in the VTS deploy directory.

docs.title: Trajectory Generation
docs.text:
GenerateTrajectories runs after compileJava. It:

  1. Calls Constants.disableHAL() so constants and trajectory generation can run on desktop without normal robot HAL behavior.
  2. Builds a Choreo robot config from DriveConstants.
  3. Ensures src/main/deploy/vts/VTS.chor exists and matches the current robot config hash.
  4. Iterates DriveTrajectories.paths.
  5. Generates or updates .traj files when path requests or robot constants change.

Running ./gradlew build is the normal way to update generated trajectory files.

docs.title: Following a Trajectory
docs.text:
AutoCommands.followTrajectory(...) loads a named Choreo trajectory and optionally resets pose to the trajectory's initial sample.

DriveTrajectory then:

  • Starts an internal timer.
  • Samples the trajectory at elapsed time.
  • Applies alliance flipping.
  • Optionally applies vertical mirroring.
  • Runs PID feedback on X, Y, and heading.
  • Adds feedforward velocities from the Choreo sample.
  • Sends field-relative chassis speeds to Drive.runVelocity.
  • Logs setpoint pose, setpoint speeds, translation error, rotation error, and whether omega override is active.

The command ends when its timer reaches the trajectory total time and then stops the drive.

docs.title: Pose Resets
docs.text:
A trajectory command can reset odometry at the start. Only reset pose when the robot's actual starting pose is known and matches the trajectory. For chained trajectories, only the first path usually resets pose.

In timidSalesman, pose reset is selected from the start-position answer before driving and launching. In monopolySalesman, the first selected trajectory starts with pose reset enabled.

docs.title: Launching During Auto
docs.text:
Auto launch behavior uses the same launcher infrastructure as teleop:

  • LaunchCalculator supplies flywheel speed, hood angle, kicker surface speed, drive angle, and other launch parameters.
  • AutoCommands.index(...) waits for flywheel readiness, delays for slamtake timing, retracts the slamtake slowly, and runs hopper/kicker launch goals.
  • AutoCommands.followTrajectoryWhileAiming(...) can override trajectory omega with launch-on-the-move aiming output when shouldAim is true.

This keeps autonomous shooting behavior aligned with teleop shooting behavior.

docs.title: Adding a New Auto Routine
docs.text:
Use this checklist:

  1. Add or update the path request in DriveTrajectories.
  2. Run ./gradlew build to generate the .traj file.
  3. Add a helper method to AutoBuilder if the routine is more than a one-liner.
  4. Register the routine in RobotContainer.configureAutos().
  5. Add AutoQuestion entries if the routine needs dashboard-selectable variants.
  6. Use existing helpers such as followTrajectory, driveToPose, index, and returnToClosestLaunchPose.
  7. Test in SIMBOT when possible.
  8. Replay logs or inspect AdvantageScope telemetry for setpoints, pose, and errors.
  9. Update this documentation if the driver station selection flow changes.

docs.title: Common Auto Failure Modes
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause What to check Runtime exception: trajectory not found Name mismatch or missing generated file. Compare the string in code to src/main/deploy/vts/*.traj. Robot starts path from wrong pose Pose reset is missing or start position answer does not match field setup. Check the selected routine questions and initial sample. Path mirrors/flips unexpectedly Alliance flip or vertical mirror path is active. Inspect DriveTrajectory/Mirror and logged trajectory poses. Launch command never indexes Flywheel, hood, drive yaw, or tilt tolerance is not satisfied. Inspect Flywheel At Goal, Hood At Goal, Drive At Goal, and pitch/roll dashboard keys. Generated files change unexpectedly Drive constants or path request hash changed. Review DriveConstants and DriveTrajectories.

docs.title: 204: Vision & Object Detection
docs.text: The vision stack combines Northstar coprocessors, AprilTag pose estimation, object detection frames, timestamped robot state, and AdvantageKit logging. Vision does not command motors directly; it publishes observations that other systems can use.

docs.title: Main Files
docs.text: File Purpose subsystems/vision/Vision.java Reads all camera IO, logs frames, filters AprilTag observations, handles object detections, and forwards accepted pose observations. subsystems/vision/VisionIO.java Defines NetworkTables connection status, AprilTag frame inputs, object detection frame inputs, recording control, and throttle control. subsystems/vision/VisionIONorthstar.java NetworkTables implementation for Northstar devices. subsystems/vision/VisionConstants.java Camera IDs, resolutions, exposure/gain, robot-to-camera transforms, filtering constants, and confidence thresholds. ObjectDetection.java Converts object detection tx/ty observations into field-relative fuel and robot translations. RobotState.java Provides timestamped pose, rotation, and slamtake-angle buffers used by vision. northstar Coprocessor-side workers and support code.

docs.title: Northstar Instances
docs.text:
VisionIONorthstar creates one NetworkTables table per device:

northstar_0
northstar_1
northstar_2
northstar_3

For each instance, robot code publishes camera configuration under config and subscribes to frame output under output.

Published configuration includes:

  • Camera ID.
  • Resolution.
  • Exposure, gain, denoise.
  • Color balance values.
  • AprilTag fiducial size.
  • Recording state.
  • Timestamp.
  • Event name, match type, and match number.
  • AprilTag layout.
  • Throttle FPS.

Subscribed outputs include:

  • observations: AprilTag frames.
  • objdetect_observations: object detection frames.
  • fps_apriltags.
  • fps_objdetect.

docs.title: Camera Configuration
docs.text:
VisionConstants.cameras is selected by robot type.

Darwin currently defines four cameras:

Index Camera ID Resolution Main use 0 40552081 1800x1200 Monochrome AprilTag camera facing rearward. 1 24737133 1280x960 Color camera with a moving pose based on slamtake angle. 2 24736167 1280x960 Color camera angled to one side. 3 24736221 1280x960 Color camera angled to the other side.

Alphabot currently defines two cameras.

Camera 1 on Darwin is special: its pose depends on RobotState.getSlamAngle(timestamp). If the slamtake angle is not available for the frame timestamp, that camera returns Optional.empty() and the frame is skipped.

docs.title: AprilTag Frame Processing
docs.text:
Vision.periodic() reads all queued frames from each camera. For each AprilTag frame:

  1. It gets the frame timestamp.
  2. It gets the camera pose at that timestamp.
  3. It skips blank frames.
  4. It decodes either a multi-tag single-pose result or a one-tag two-pose ambiguous result.
  5. For ambiguous one-tag results, it chooses the pose whose rotation is closer to the current estimated robot rotation if the ambiguity ratio is acceptable.
  6. It rejects poses outside the field border margin.
  7. It rejects poses outside allowed Z bounds.
  8. It finds tag poses in the selected AprilTag layout.
  9. It calculates average camera-to-tag distance.
  10. It computes XY and theta standard deviations.
  11. It creates a RobotState.VisionObservation.

Accepted observations are sorted by timestamp and sent to:

RobotState.getInstance().addVisionObservation(...)

docs.title: Vision Filtering Constants
docs.text:
Important constants in VisionConstants:

Constant Meaning ambiguityThreshold Used to reject or choose between ambiguous one-tag pose solutions. fieldBorderMargin Allowed margin outside field before rejecting a robot pose. zMin, zMax Allowed robot Z range for accepted vision poses. xyStdDevCoefficient Scales XY measurement uncertainty. thetaStdDevCoefficient Scales rotation measurement uncertainty. targetLogTimeSecs How long recently detected tag poses remain visible in logs.

The standard deviation model increases uncertainty with distance and decreases uncertainty with more detected tags.

docs.title: AprilTag Layout Selection
docs.text:
RobotContainer creates a logged dashboard chooser named AprilTag Layout. Options include:

  • Official.
  • Hub.
  • Tower.
  • Outpost.
  • None.

The selected layout is passed to Vision and published to Northstar. RobotContainer raises an alert when a non-default layout is selected.

AprilTag layout JSON files live under:

src/main/deploy/apriltags

They are grouped by layout source/type such as andymark, hq, and welded.

docs.title: Object Detection Frames
docs.text:
Object detection frames are arrays of detections. Each detection uses 10 values:

  1. Class ID.
  2. Confidence.
  3. Corner 0 tx.
  4. Corner 0 ty.
  5. Corner 1 tx.
  6. Corner 1 ty.
  7. Corner 2 tx.
  8. Corner 2 ty.
  9. Corner 3 tx.
  10. Corner 3 ty.

The current class IDs are:

  • 0: fuel.
  • 1: robot.

Northstar enforces the confidence threshold, so the robot-side thresholds currently default to 0.0.

docs.title: Fuel Position Tracking
docs.text:
ObjectDetection stores fuel positions in a spatial hash grid with 1-meter cells. The flow is:

  1. Autonomous initializes assumed starting fuel positions.
  2. Vision clears old fuel and fuel inside the robot intake footprint.
  3. For the most recent object detection frame from a camera, vision clears fuel in that camera's field of view.
  4. New fuel observations are projected to the field using robot pose, camera pose, and corner tx/ty data.
  5. Fuel outside the field is rejected.
  6. Nearby fuel records are deduplicated using a tunable overlap distance.

In real mode, fuel observations are ignored outside autonomous. In SIMBOT mode, ObjectDetection.getFuelTranslations() returns fuel from FuelSim.

docs.title: Robot Position Tracking
docs.text:
Robot detections use the lower bounding-box corners to estimate the top-down position of another robot. They are projected through the same timestamped pose and camera-pose process, then deduplicated using ObjectDetection/RobotOverlap.

Recent robot translations are logged by Robot.robotPeriodic() as 3D poses for visualization.

docs.title: Recording and Throttling
docs.text:
Vision publishes recording and throttle requests to Northstar:

  • Recording is enabled when FMS has been attached long enough, when /SmartDashboard/Enable Recording is true, or when alwaysRecord is true.
  • Throttle is set to 1.0 FPS when Robot.shouldThrottle() returns true.
  • Otherwise throttle is set to -1.0, meaning no requested throttle.

Robot.shouldThrottle() becomes true after the robot has been disabled for 5 seconds.

docs.title: Disconnect Alerts
docs.text:
Each Northstar instance has a disconnect timer. Alerts distinguish between:

  • Northstar disconnected from NetworkTables.
  • Northstar connected to NetworkTables but not publishing frames.

If no AprilTag or object detection frames arrive for 1.5 seconds, the instance is treated as disconnected or stale.

docs.title: Adding or Moving a Camera
docs.text:
Use this checklist:

  1. Add or update the CameraConfig in VisionConstants.
  2. Set the correct camera ID, resolution, exposure, gain, denoise, color balance, FOV, and stdDevFactor.
  3. Define the camera pose as a function of timestamp.
  4. If the camera moves, ensure the required mechanism state is buffered in RobotState.
  5. Update Northstar device setup so the expected camera ID is attached to the expected instance.
  6. Test NetworkTables connectivity and frame publishing.
  7. Check AprilTagVision/Inst*/RobotPose, TagPoses, latency, and FPS in AdvantageScope.
  8. Update this page and any hardware port notes.

docs.title: Object Detection Model Note
docs.text: The historical CoreML object detection models for Rebuilt are documented in the repository's original notes and are AGPL-3.0 licensed. Keep model licensing visible when adding or distributing object detection assets.

docs.title: 205: Simulation & Replay
docs.text:
Simulation and replay are separate workflows. They both run on desktop, but they answer different questions.

Workflow Robot type/mode Use it for Physics simulation SIMBOT / SIM Testing commands, mechanism logic, fuel sim, basic driver behavior, and dashboard outputs without a robot. Log replay DARWIN or ALPHABOT on desktop / REPLAY Testing deterministic logic changes against recorded real or simulated logs. Real robot DARWIN or ALPHABOT on RoboRIO / REAL Practice, tuning, competition, and hardware validation.

docs.title: Running Physics Simulation
docs.text:
Run:

SIMBOT=1 ./gradlew simulateJava

Constants.getRobot() selects SIMBOT on desktop only when the SIMBOT environment variable is present. Constants.getMode() then returns SIM.

SIMBOT currently creates:

  • Drive with four ModuleIOSim instances and no-op gyro IO.
  • Slamtake with SlamIOSim and RollerSystemIOSim.
  • Hopper with RollerSystemIOSim and simulated fuel count.
  • Kicker with no-op roller IO for the current branch plus simulated fuel count.
  • Leds with LedsIOHAL.
  • FuelSim for field fuel state.

Any subsystem not explicitly created for SIMBOT falls back to a no-op IO implementation.

docs.title: Simulation GUI
docs.text:
The sim directory contains:

  • simgui.json.
  • simgui-ds.json.
  • simgui-window.json.
  • Simulation Key Mapping.html.

Copy the JSON files into the project root to use the provided WPILib simulation GUI layout. Open the HTML file to see keyboard mappings for the simulated Xbox and override controllers.

The sim GUI is disabled by default in build.gradle:

wpi.sim.addGui().defaultEnabled = false
wpi.sim.addDriverstation()

This keeps command-line replay workflows cleaner while still enabling the Driver Station simulator.

docs.title: FuelSim
docs.text:
RobotContainer.configureFuelSim() registers:

  • The robot footprint.
  • The intake footprint.
  • A predicate for when intake is active.
  • A callback for increasing stored fuel.

SIMBOT starts with 8 fuel stored in SimFuelCount. The simulated capacity is 80, and the simulated launch rate is 16 fuel per second. In autonomous, fuel sim clears and respawns starting fuel, then resets stored fuel to 8.

ObjectDetection.getFuelTranslations() returns FuelSim fuel positions in SIMBOT mode.

docs.title: Running Replay
docs.text:
Run a desktop simulation without SIMBOT:

./gradlew simulateJava

Because the default robot is DARWIN, desktop execution uses REPLAY. AdvantageKit finds a replay log, reads it, and writes a new log with _sim appended.

Replay should not require real hardware IO. RobotContainer creates no-op implementations for any subsystem that was not created by the selected robot path.

docs.title: What Replay Can Catch
docs.text:
Replay is useful for:

  • Checking whether a logic change would have changed an old match.
  • Testing vision filtering changes.
  • Reviewing auto or drive command behavior against recorded pose data.
  • Verifying logged outputs and dashboard calculations.
  • Debugging state-machine behavior without the robot.

Replay is not enough for:

  • Validating motor controller configuration.
  • Validating CAN IDs or wiring.
  • Measuring real mechanism response.
  • Tuning feedforward values that depend on real hardware.
  • Checking camera exposure or physical calibration.

docs.title: AdvantageScope Signals to Inspect
docs.text:
When testing simulation or replay, inspect:

Signal Why it matters SwerveStates/Measured Whether simulated or replayed modules report believable state. SwerveStates/Setpoints Whether commands are requesting the expected module states. RobotState/EstimatedPose Main pose estimate after odometry and vision fusion. AprilTagVision/RobotPoses All accepted AprilTag-derived robot poses. DriveTrajectory/TranslationError Path-following error. DriveTrajectory/RotationError Heading error while following paths. Flywheel/Setpoint and Flywheel/Goal Whether launcher control is tracking the desired target. Hood/Profile/GoalPositionRad Hood target angle. ObjectDetection/FuelTranslations Simulated or detected fuel positions. CommandsAll/* Which command names are active. Throttled Whether disabled throttling is active.

docs.title: Replay-Safe Code Checklist
docs.text:
Before merging subsystem or command changes, check:

  • Hardware reads happen only in updateInputs.
  • Subsystem logic uses the inputs object, not vendor classes.
  • Outputs are applied in the IO layer.
  • Any new input field is included in an @AutoLog inputs class.
  • New commands declare subsystem requirements.
  • New runtime decisions record enough telemetry to debug later.
  • Sim and replay behavior are intentional when hardware IO is missing.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Simulation and Replay
docs.text:
Symptom Likely cause Fix Simulation launches replay file chooser SIMBOT environment variable is missing. Run SIMBOT=1 ./gradlew simulateJava. No mechanism moves in SIMBOT The subsystem may be using no-op IO. Check RobotContainer for a SIMBOT implementation. Logs are missing live NT4 data in replay Replay writes a WPILOG output instead of publishing all live data. Open the generated _sim log in AdvantageScope. Fuel count does not change Intake predicate or launch goal is not active. Check slamtake goals, kicker goal, and Hopper/SimFueldCount. Driver controls do nothing in sim Sim DS/controller mappings may not be configured. Copy the sim JSON files and use the HTML key map.

Hopper/SimFueldCount is the current logged key in code, including the spelling. If the code is renamed later, update saved layouts and this troubleshooting table together.

docs.title: 301: System Design
docs.text: This page explains the higher-level design decisions that connect robot lifecycle, subsystem structure, logging, replay, simulation, and operator workflows.

docs.title: Architecture Diagram
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Entry["Robot program entry"]
        Main["Main.java"]
        Robot["Robot.java<br/>Lifecycle, logging, scheduler"]
        Container["RobotContainer.java<br/>Composition, controls, autos"]
    end

    subgraph Behavior["Behavior layer"]
        Commands["Commands<br/>Drive, launch, compact, auto"]
        Subsystems["Subsystems<br/>Drive, launcher, intake, hopper, kicker, vision, LEDs"]
        State["RobotState<br/>Pose, velocity, buffers, vision fusion"]
    end

    subgraph Boundary["Hardware abstraction boundary"]
        IO["IO interfaces<br/>Logged inputs and requested outputs"]
        Sim["Simulation IO<br/>Physics models and simulated sensors"]
        Real["Real IO<br/>Vendor hardware and device config"]
        Replay["Replay/no-op IO<br/>Logged inputs drive behavior"]
    end

    subgraph External["External services"]
        Vision["Vision / Northstar"]
    end

    Main --> Robot --> Container
    Container --> Commands
    Container --> Subsystems
    Commands --> Subsystems
    Subsystems --> IO
    Subsystems --> State
    IO --> Sim
    IO --> Real
    IO --> Replay
    Vision --> State

    classDef entry fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#5e35b1,color:#111827
    classDef behavior fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#111827
    classDef boundary fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#111827
    classDef external fill:#ffedd5,stroke:#c2410c,color:#111827

    class Main,Robot,Container entry
    class Commands,Subsystems,State behavior
    class IO,Sim,Real,Replay boundary
    class Vision external

docs.title: Composition Root
docs.text:
RobotContainer is the composition root. It owns:

  • Subsystem fields.
  • Controller objects.
  • Override switch triggers.
  • Alerts.
  • Auto selector and AprilTag layout chooser.
  • SIMBOT fuel sim.
  • Default commands.
  • Button bindings.

This keeps construction and wiring in one place. Subsystem classes should not create controller objects, auto selectors, or unrelated mechanisms.

docs.title: Mode Selection
docs.text:
Mode selection starts in Constants:

public static Mode getMode() {
  return switch (getRobot()) {
    case DARWIN, ALPHABOT -> RobotBase.isReal() ? Mode.REAL : Mode.REPLAY;
    case SIMBOT -> Mode.SIM;
  };
}

That design intentionally makes desktop Darwin/Alphabot runs use replay. Full physics simulation requires SIMBOT.

docs.title: Data Flow Per Loop
docs.text:
The normal loop flow is:

  1. Robot.robotPeriodic() resets cycle timing.
  2. Global battery inputs are logged.
  3. VirtualSubsystem.runAllPeriodic() runs virtual subsystems such as vision and LEDs.
  4. WPILib's CommandScheduler runs.
  5. VirtualSubsystem.runAllPeriodicAfterScheduler() runs virtual after-scheduler hooks.
  6. FullSubsystem.runAllPeriodicAfterScheduler() applies normal subsystem outputs.
  7. Battery logger and global telemetry finish.
  8. Mechanism3d, object detection, hub shift, launch parameters, and dashboard outputs are recorded.

Subsystems that extend FullSubsystem can split input/logic and output application across the scheduler boundary.

docs.title: State Ownership
docs.text:
Use these ownership rules:

Data Owner Hardware sensor readings IO inputs class. Mechanism goals Owning subsystem. Controller bindings RobotContainer. Robot pose estimate RobotState. Auto routine selection AutoSelector and RobotContainer. Autonomous routine composition AutoBuilder and AutoCommands. Vision frame processing Vision. Object-derived fuel/robot positions ObjectDetection. Driver dashboard output RobotContainer or subsystem closest to the value.

Avoid duplicate sources of truth. If two classes need the same robot-wide state, it probably belongs in RobotState or a focused utility.

docs.title: Hardware Boundary
docs.text:
Subsystems should not import vendor motor-controller or sensor classes. Hardware details belong in IO implementations. The boundary gives the project:

  • Replayable logs.
  • Easier simulation.
  • Cleaner testing.
  • More focused code review.
  • Safer hardware bring-up.

When a subsystem needs a new measured value, add it to the IO input class and populate it in each implementation.

docs.title: Simulation Boundary
docs.text:
Simulation should use the same subsystem logic as the real robot. The simulator should differ only behind IO implementations or in simulation-only services such as FuelSim.

Good simulation behavior:

  • Responds to applied voltage or setpoint requests.
  • Uses WPILib physics models where practical.
  • Publishes plausible current, position, and velocity.
  • Leaves subsystem logic unchanged.

Weak simulation behavior:

  • Hardcodes sensor values that do not respond to outputs.
  • Bypasses subsystem state machines.
  • Changes command logic only for simulation.

docs.title: Vision and Timestamping
docs.text:
Vision data is timestamped and may arrive later than the current robot loop. RobotState keeps interpolation buffers so AprilTag and object detection observations can be applied at the time the camera frame was captured.

Important buffers:

  • Pose buffer for odometry pose.
  • Rotation buffer for roll/pitch/yaw.
  • Slam angle buffer for moving camera pose.

This is why mechanisms that affect camera transforms must publish timestamped observations to RobotState.

docs.title: Autonomous Design
docs.text:
Autonomous is designed around reusable command helpers and dashboard questions:

  • AutoSelector handles selection and responses.
  • AutoBuilder converts responses into complete routines.
  • AutoCommands provides reusable primitives.
  • DriveTrajectory follows generated paths.
  • DriveTrajectories and GenerateTrajectories own path generation.

Do not bury dashboard reads inside low-level commands. Read selected responses through suppliers passed into auto-building code.

docs.title: Performance and Loop Timing
docs.text:
The robot loop is configured for 0.02 seconds. The watchdog timeout is widened to 0.2 seconds, and the command scheduler period is set to the same watchdog value. That reduces noise from transient overrun warnings, but it does not make slow code safe.

Hot paths should avoid:

  • Large allocations every loop.
  • Blocking operations.
  • File or network IO in periodic methods.
  • Repeated expensive stream operations on large data.
  • Long synchronous hardware calls.

Use LoggedTracer to understand cycle-time impact when changing periodic behavior.

docs.title: Design Review Questions
docs.text:
Ask these before merging a substantial change:

  • Does the change preserve replayability?
  • Is each piece of state owned by one class?
  • Are new hardware reads logged as inputs?
  • Are outputs applied after scheduler where appropriate?
  • Is simulation behavior representative enough for the feature?
  • Are dashboard controls logged if they affect behavior?
  • Does the feature fail safely when disconnected, disabled, or unzeroed?
  • Are autonomous and teleop using shared behavior where they should?
  • Can someone debug this from logs after an event?

docs.title: 302: Logging, Dashboard, and Telemetry
docs.text: This page explains what the code logs, where dashboard outputs come from, and how to add telemetry without breaking replayability.

docs.title: AdvantageKit Data Paths
docs.text:
Robot.java configures the data path based on Constants.getMode():

Mode Receivers/source REAL WPILOGWriter and NT4Publisher. SIM NT4Publisher. REPLAY WPILOGReader source and _sim WPILOGWriter.

Metadata recorded at startup includes:

  • Project name.
  • Build date.
  • Git SHA.
  • Git date.
  • Git branch.
  • Git dirty state.
  • Hostname.
  • Platform.

This metadata should be used when comparing logs from different robot builds.

docs.title: Input Logging
docs.text:
Use Logger.processInputs(...) for IO input objects generated by @AutoLog. Examples:

Logger.processInputs("Drive/Gyro", gyroInputs);
Logger.processInputs("Drive/Module" + index, inputs);
Logger.processInputs("Vision/Inst" + i, inputs[i]);
Logger.processInputs("Flywheel", inputs);

Inputs should represent measured state. If the value comes from a sensor, motor controller status signal, NetworkTables camera frame, or simulated sensor, it belongs in an inputs class.

docs.title: Output Logging
docs.text:
Use Logger.recordOutput(...) for derived values, setpoints, visualization objects, and command state. Examples in this project include:

  • SwerveStates/Setpoints.
  • SwerveChassisSpeeds/Measured.
  • DriveTrajectory/Setpoint/Pose.
  • AprilTagVision/RobotPoses.
  • ObjectDetection/FuelTranslations.
  • HubShift/Official.
  • LaunchCalculator/Parameters.
  • CommandsAll/<name>.

When adding an output, prefer names that group naturally in AdvantageScope.

docs.title: AutoLogOutput
docs.text:
Use @AutoLogOutput for values that can be computed from object state and should be logged every loop. Examples:

  • Drive.getModuleStates().
  • Flywheel.atGoal.
  • Hood.getMeasuredAngleRad().
  • TrashCompactor.getMeasuredHeightMeters().
  • RobotState.estimatedPose.

Do not hide expensive calculations behind @AutoLogOutput. If it allocates heavily or does nontrivial work, consider logging explicitly where the data is already available.

docs.title: Command Logging
docs.text:
Robot registers command scheduler callbacks. For every command initialize, finish, or interrupt, it records:

  • CommandsUnique/<name>_<hash> for individual command instances.
  • CommandsAll/<name> for whether any command with that name is active.

This is useful when debugging conflicts, interrupts, default commands, and button behavior.

docs.title: LoggedTracer
docs.text:
LoggedTracer records cycle timing checkpoints. The main loop resets it at the start of robotPeriodic() and records sections such as:

  • BatteryLogger/Periodic.
  • Robot/Commands.
  • Robot/AfterScheduler.
  • ObjectDetection/ClearOldFuelPoses.
  • Drive/Periodic.
  • Vision/Periodic.

Use it sparingly for runtime sections where loop timing matters.

docs.title: SmartDashboard Outputs
docs.text:
SmartDashboard is used for driver-facing outputs and selectors, not as a replacement for logs.

Examples:

  • Auto routine and question choosers.
  • AprilTag layout chooser.
  • Drive At Goal.
  • Drive Pitch & Roll OK.
  • Match Time.
  • Hub shift status.
  • Hood Angle Offset.
  • Flywheel Speed.
  • Flywheel At Goal.
  • Hood At Goal.
  • Trash Compactor At Min Height.

If the value is needed for post-match debugging, log it with AdvantageKit too.

docs.title: Logged Dashboard Inputs
docs.text:
The code uses AdvantageKit dashboard wrappers such as:

  • LoggedDashboardChooser for auto routines and AprilTag layout selection.
  • LoggedNetworkNumber for /SmartDashboard/Auto/Offset Time?.
  • LoggedNetworkBoolean for /SmartDashboard/Enable Recording.

These wrappers make operator selections visible in logs.

docs.title: Battery and Current Logging
docs.text:
Robot logs battery voltage, RoboRIO current, and Mac Mini current through BatteryLogger. Subsystems report current usage with stable keys, such as:

  • FullDrive/Drive/<index>.
  • FullDrive/Turn/<index>.
  • Flywheel.
  • Hood.
  • TrashCompactor.
  • Roller system names.

Current usage is used both for telemetry and for understanding performance throttling or brownout risk.

docs.title: Naming Guidance
docs.text:
Use stable paths:

  • Good: DriveTrajectory/TranslationError.
  • Good: Flywheel/Feedforward.
  • Good: Vision/ObjDetect/Inst2.
  • Avoid: Debug, Temp, MyValue, or names tied to one temporary experiment.

Use units in the name when the unit is not obvious:

  • Hood/Profile/GoalVelocityRadPerSec.
  • TrashCompactor/SetpointVelocityMetersPerSec.
  • DriveCommands/Launching/YawToleranceDeg.

docs.title: Adding Telemetry Checklist
docs.text:
1. Decide whether the value is an input, derived output, dashboard value, or tunable.
  2. Put measured hardware state in an @AutoLog inputs class.
  3. Put derived state or setpoints under Logger.recordOutput.
  4. Put driver-facing values on SmartDashboard only when drivers or pit crew need them live.
  5. Use LoggedDashboardChooser, LoggedNetworkNumber, or LoggedNetworkBoolean for logged dashboard inputs.
  6. Avoid adding noisy high-frequency arrays unless they are useful in AdvantageScope.
  7. Run simulation or replay and verify the path appears where expected.

docs.title: 401: Pull Request Review Guide
docs.text: This guide is for mentors and student leads reviewing robot-code pull requests. Review should focus on behavior, replayability, safety, diagnostics, and maintainability.

docs.title: Review Priorities
docs.text:
Review in this order:

  1. Safety and robot damage risk.
  2. Replayability and architecture boundaries.
  3. Runtime behavior and command scheduling.
  4. Simulation/replay coverage.
  5. Logging and dashboard visibility.
  6. Formatting, naming, and cleanup.

A pretty diff with broken robot semantics is still broken. Start with behavior.

docs.title: AdvantageKit IO Pattern
docs.text:
Check:

  • Subsystems do not import vendor hardware classes.
  • Hardware reads happen in updateInputs.
  • Measured values are stored in @AutoLog inputs.
  • Subsystem logic reads from input objects.
  • Output requests are represented as output fields, modes, or setpoint methods.
  • Replay has a no-op path or uses logged inputs.

Red flags:

  • Calling motor.getPosition() in a subsystem.
  • Reading back desired outputs as if they were measured state.
  • Adding a new sensor value without logging it.
  • Adding real hardware code without a sim or no-op fallback.

docs.title: Command-Based Behavior
docs.text:
Check:

  • Commands declare requirements for every subsystem they control.
  • Commands do not block with Thread.sleep() or long loops.
  • Constructor code does not capture state that should be read at schedule time.
  • State reset happens in initialize() or through command factories as appropriate.
  • Default commands do not fight button commands.
  • run, runEnd, startEnd, deadlineFor, raceWith, and finallyDo are used intentionally.

For button bindings, inspect how triggers compose. Complex trigger chains should still be readable and testable.

docs.title: Mode and Constants Safety
docs.text:
Before merge:

  • Constants.robot should be DARWIN.
  • Constants.tuningMode should be false.
  • SIMBOT should not be deployable.
  • Any temporary event, testing, or characterization constants should be removed or isolated.

checkConstantsPullRequest exists to catch the most important merge mistakes, but reviewers should still read the diff.

docs.title: Simulation and Replay
docs.text:
Ask:

  • Can this behavior be checked in SIMBOT?
  • Can this logic be replayed from logs?
  • Does the code behave intentionally when an IO implementation is no-op?
  • Are simulated inputs plausible enough to exercise the subsystem state machine?

Simulation does not need to be perfect for every mechanism, but it should be honest. A sim class that always reports "at goal" can hide real bugs.

docs.title: Logging and Diagnostics
docs.text:
Every meaningful behavior change should leave enough telemetry to debug after the robot leaves the field.

Check for:

  • Logged inputs for measured values.
  • Logged outputs for setpoints, goals, errors, and selected modes.
  • Alerts for hardware disconnects or unsafe operator selections.
  • Dashboard values only where operators need live feedback.
  • Stable AdvantageScope paths.
  • Units in names when unclear.

If reviewers cannot tell why a command did or did not run from a log, ask for better telemetry.

docs.title: Autonomous Review
docs.text:
For auto changes, check:

  • Path names match files under src/main/deploy/vts.
  • Generated trajectories are committed when expected.
  • Pose reset behavior is intentional.
  • Alliance flipping and mirroring are understood.
  • Auto question responses have sensible defaults.
  • Launch/indexing conditions cannot deadlock silently.
  • Drive and mechanism commands have correct requirements.
  • New dashboard choices are documented.

Test auto changes in SIMBOT whenever possible.

docs.title: Vision Review
docs.text:
For vision changes, check:

  • Camera transforms are in robot coordinates and documented.
  • Moving camera transforms use timestamped mechanism state.
  • Frame parsing matches Northstar output format.
  • Rejection filters are explicit.
  • Standard deviations are plausible.
  • AprilTag layout selection remains logged.
  • Object detections are bounded to field geometry.
  • Disconnected/stale cameras create useful alerts.

Small sign errors in camera transforms can look plausible, so require AdvantageScope screenshots or logs for calibration-sensitive changes when possible.

docs.title: Performance Review
docs.text:
Look for hot-loop problems:

  • New object allocation in periodic() or execute() without a reason.
  • Large stream operations every loop.
  • Network or file IO in periodic code.
  • Reflection in periodic code.
  • Recreating controllers, filters, or profiles every loop instead of only when tunables change.
  • Unbounded lists or maps that grow during a match.

Use LoggedTracer if the change could affect loop time.

docs.title: Hardware Review
docs.text:
For hardware IO additions, check:

  • CAN IDs, bus names, inversion, offsets, and reductions match electrical/mechanical docs.
  • Neutral modes are set intentionally.
  • Current limits are configured.
  • Status signal frequencies are selected intentionally.
  • Faults and connection status are logged.
  • Disabled behavior is safe.
  • Homing and zeroing cannot drive indefinitely at damaging output.

Real hardware code should fail loudly in logs and alerts when a device is missing.

docs.title: Documentation Review
docs.text:
Ask for docs updates when a PR changes:

  • Setup steps.
  • Controls.
  • Dashboard keys.
  • Autonomous routine choices.
  • Subsystem behavior.
  • Hardware IDs, ports, or camera placement.
  • Simulation or replay workflow.
  • Contributor expectations.

Documentation does not need to describe every line of code, but it should keep operational knowledge out of private memory.

docs.title: Final Reviewer Checklist
docs.text:
• Build passes.
  • Spotless has run.
  • Constants are merge-safe.
  • New behavior is logged.
  • Commands have correct requirements.
  • Simulation/replay story is clear.
  • Documentation is updated for user-visible or workflow-visible changes.
  • No unrelated refactors are mixed into the PR.

docs.title: 402: Hardware Reference Notes
docs.text: This page collects hardware-adjacent notes that affect software setup, logging, and debugging. It is not a wiring bible; verify final IDs and wiring against the robot electrical documentation before making hardware changes.

docs.title: Robot Selection
docs.text:
Constants.RobotType currently includes:

  • DARWIN.
  • ALPHABOT.
  • SIMBOT.

The default selected robot is DARWIN. SIMBOT is selected on desktop only when the SIMBOT environment variable is present. Deploying with SIMBOT selected is blocked by checkConstantsDeploy.

docs.title: Darwin Drive Constants
docs.text:
Selected values from DriveConstants:

Constant Value Track width X 21.25 in Track width Y 22.25 in Frame width X 26.5 in Frame width Y 27.5 in Full width X 33.0 in Full width Y 34.0 in Max linear speed 4.2 m/s Max trajectory linear speed 4.0 m/s Max angular speed 5.374 rad/s Wheel radius 1.93045163 in Trajectory wheel radius 2.0 in Mass 150 lb Wheel coefficient of friction 1.5 Rotation MOI 6.0 kg*m^2 Module type SDS MK5i, R1 reduction with MK5n notes on rear turn reductions

docs.title: Darwin Drive CAN IDs
docs.text:
Device ID Gyro 30 Front-left drive motor 0 Front-right drive motor 11 Back-left drive motor 23 Back-right drive motor 13 Front-left turn motor 1 Front-right turn motor 10 Back-left turn motor 22 Back-right turn motor 12 Front-left encoder 42 Front-right encoder 40 Back-left encoder 43 Back-right encoder 41

Darwin drive constants use CAN bus "*".

docs.title: Alphabot Drive Notes
docs.text: Alphabot constants are also present. Alphabot uses SDS MK4i modules with L3 reduction, a 28 in frame, 34.5 in full width, and 4.69 m/s max linear speed. Keep Alphabot constants intact unless the change is intentionally shared.

docs.title: Mac Mini Port Mapping
docs.text:
Location IDs represent USB 2 devices connected directly to the port. They are not accurate for USB 3 devices or for devices connected through hubs.

Top-down view:

Front Ports Back Ports USB 0x02120000 Thunderbolt 0x03100000 USB 0x02110000 Thunderbolt 0x00100000 Thunderbolt 0x01100000 HDMI Status light Ethernet Headphone jack AC input

Bottom-up view:

Front Ports Back Ports Headphone jack AC input Status light Ethernet HDMI Thunderbolt 0x01100000 USB 0x02110000 Thunderbolt 0x00100000 USB 0x02120000 Thunderbolt 0x03100000

Keep camera enumeration stable by documenting which physical camera is connected to which port.

docs.title: Phoenix 6 CAN Frame Reference
docs.text:
The original Phoenix frame notes in this repository were based only on public Phoenix APIs, not reverse engineering. They were used as a reference for optimizing status signal frequencies.

Recorded API/device versions:

  • Phoenix API 25.4.0.
  • TalonFX 25.2.1.0.
  • Pigeon 25.5.0.0.
  • CANcoder 25.5.1.0.

For CAN optimization, prioritize the signals the code actually reads into IO inputs:

  • Drive and turn position.
  • Drive and turn velocity.
  • Applied voltage.
  • Supply and torque current.
  • Temperature.
  • Connection/fault status.
