  • Gyro yaw, pitch, and roll.
  • CANcoder absolute/relative position where used.

Reduce or slow unnecessary status signals on real hardware implementations to avoid bus saturation.

docs.title: Hardware Implementation Checklist
docs.text:
When adding a real IO implementation:

  1. Implement the existing IO interface.
  2. Populate every logged input field with real measured data or a clear default.
  3. Apply every output mode intentionally.
  4. Configure neutral mode, current limits, inversion, sensor offsets, PID gains, and feedforward in one place.
  5. Set status signal frequencies based on what the robot actually logs and controls.
  6. Report connection status accurately.
  7. Preserve replay behavior by keeping hardware APIs out of the subsystem class.
  8. Test disabled behavior, enabled behavior, and brownout/current behavior.
  9. Add or update hardware notes when IDs, ports, offsets, or assumptions change.

docs.title: Cookbook: Add a New Subsystem
docs.text: Use this cookbook when adding a mechanism that should follow the AdvantageKit IO pattern. The example uses an elevator, but the same structure applies to rollers, pivots, climbers, and other mechanisms.

docs.title: 1. Decide the Ownership Boundary
docs.text:
Before creating files, decide:

  • What physical mechanism or service owns the state?
  • What measured values are required?
  • What commands or goals will the subsystem expose?
  • What outputs must be applied to hardware?
  • What should happen when disabled, unzeroed, disconnected, or in replay?
  • What simulation fidelity is needed?

If two mechanisms must always act together, they may belong in one subsystem. If they have separate state, separate hardware, and separate commands, keep them separate.

docs.title: 2. Create the IO Interface
docs.text:
Create an interface in the subsystem package:

public interface ElevatorIO {
  @AutoLog
  public static class ElevatorIOInputs {
    public boolean connected = false;
    public double positionMeters = 0.0;
    public double velocityMetersPerSec = 0.0;
    public double appliedVolts = 0.0;
    public double supplyCurrentAmps = 0.0;
    public double tempCelsius = 0.0;
  }

  public static enum ElevatorIOOutputMode {
    COAST,
    BRAKE,
    OPEN_LOOP,
    CLOSED_LOOP
  }

  public static class ElevatorIOOutputs {
    public ElevatorIOOutputMode mode = ElevatorIOOutputMode.BRAKE;
    public double appliedVolts = 0.0;
    public double positionMeters = 0.0;
    public double velocityMetersPerSec = 0.0;
    public double feedforwardVolts = 0.0;
    public double kP = 0.0;
    public double kD = 0.0;
  }

  public default void updateInputs(ElevatorIOInputs inputs) {}

  public default void applyOutputs(ElevatorIOOutputs outputs) {}
}

Use inputs for measured state only. Use outputs for requests.

docs.title: 3. Create the Subsystem
docs.text:
The subsystem owns the IO object, generated inputs, outputs, goals, alerts, and commands.

public class Elevator extends FullSubsystem {
  private final ElevatorIO io;
  private final ElevatorIOInputsAutoLogged inputs = new ElevatorIOInputsAutoLogged();
  private final ElevatorIOOutputs outputs = new ElevatorIOOutputs();

  private final Alert disconnected =
      new Alert("Elevator motor disconnected!", Alert.AlertType.kError);

  private double goalMeters = 0.0;

  public Elevator(ElevatorIO io) {
    this.io = io;
  }

  @Override
  public void periodic() {
    io.updateInputs(inputs);
    Logger.processInputs("Elevator", inputs);

    disconnected.set(Robot.showHardwareAlerts() && !inputs.connected);

    if (DriverStation.isDisabled()) {
      outputs.mode = ElevatorIOOutputMode.BRAKE;
    }
  }

  @Override
  public void periodicAfterScheduler() {
    if (DriverStation.isEnabled()) {
      outputs.mode = ElevatorIOOutputMode.CLOSED_LOOP;
      outputs.positionMeters = goalMeters;
    }

    io.applyOutputs(outputs);
  }

  public Command runToHeight(DoubleSupplier meters) {
    return run(() -> goalMeters = meters.getAsDouble());
  }
}

Use FullSubsystem when the subsystem needs after-scheduler output application. Use VirtualSubsystem for non-command-required services such as vision or LEDs.

docs.title: 4. Add Simulation IO
docs.text:
Use WPILib simulation classes where possible.

public class ElevatorIOSim implements ElevatorIO {
  private final ElevatorSim sim = new ElevatorSim(...);
  private double appliedVolts = 0.0;

  @Override
  public void updateInputs(ElevatorIOInputs inputs) {
    sim.setInputVoltage(MathUtil.clamp(appliedVolts, -12.0, 12.0));
    sim.update(Constants.loopPeriodSecs);

    inputs.connected = true;
    inputs.positionMeters = sim.getPositionMeters();
    inputs.velocityMetersPerSec = sim.getVelocityMetersPerSecond();
    inputs.appliedVolts = appliedVolts;
    inputs.supplyCurrentAmps = Math.abs(sim.getCurrentDrawAmps());
  }

  @Override
  public void applyOutputs(ElevatorIOOutputs outputs) {
    switch (outputs.mode) {
      case COAST, BRAKE -> appliedVolts = 0.0;
      case OPEN_LOOP -> appliedVolts = outputs.appliedVolts;
      case CLOSED_LOOP -> {
        // Run a simulated controller here or use a simple profiled model.
      }
    }
  }
}

Simulation should react to output requests. Avoid returning fixed sensor values unless the mechanism truly cannot move in the test.

docs.title: 5. Add Real IO
docs.text:
When hardware is ready, create a real implementation such as ElevatorIOTalonFX or ElevatorIOSparkMax.

Real IO should:

  • Configure motor controllers in the constructor.
  • Apply current limits and neutral modes.
  • Set inversion and sensor conversion factors.
  • Set status signal rates.
  • Read all measured values in updateInputs.
  • Apply output modes in applyOutputs.
  • Report connection status accurately.
  • Avoid subsystem-specific decisions.

If real hardware is not ready, leave the subsystem wired to no-op IO for that robot path and document the gap.

docs.title: 6. Wire It in RobotContainer
docs.text:
Instantiate the subsystem based on robot/mode. Follow existing patterns and preserve no-op replay behavior.

switch (Constants.getRobot()) {
  case SIMBOT -> elevator = new Elevator(new ElevatorIOSim());
  case DARWIN -> {
    // Use real IO when implemented.
  }
  case ALPHABOT -> {
    // Use real or no-op IO as appropriate.
  }
}

if (elevator == null) {
  elevator = new Elevator(new ElevatorIO() {});
}

Then configure:

  • Default command.
  • Button bindings.
  • Auto usage.
  • Dashboard outputs.
  • Coast overrides if applicable.

docs.title: 7. Add Commands
docs.text:
Prefer command factory methods on the subsystem or in a focused command utility class. Commands should declare requirements by using subsystem methods such as run, runEnd, or by passing the subsystem to Commands.run.

public Command holdHome() {
  return run(() -> goalMeters = 0.0);
}

public Command runOpenLoop(DoubleSupplier volts) {
  return runEnd(
      () -> {
        outputs.mode = ElevatorIOOutputMode.OPEN_LOOP;
        outputs.appliedVolts = volts.getAsDouble();
      },
      () -> outputs.mode = ElevatorIOOutputMode.BRAKE);
}

Do not block in commands.

docs.title: 8. Add Logging and Alerts
docs.text:
At minimum, log:

  • Inputs through Logger.processInputs.
  • Goal state.
  • Setpoint state.
  • Measured state if not already obvious from inputs.
  • At-goal booleans.
  • Mode enums.

Add alerts for disconnected hardware and unsafe configuration.

docs.title: 9. Test
docs.text:
Use this sequence:

  1. ./gradlew build.
  2. SIMBOT=1 ./gradlew simulateJava if simulation is wired.
  3. Replay a log if the behavior can be checked from logged inputs.
  4. Confirm telemetry appears in AdvantageScope.
  5. Test disabled behavior before enabled behavior on hardware.
  6. Test homing/zeroing at low risk before closed-loop movement.

docs.title: Review Checklist
docs.text:
• IO boundary is clean.
  • Inputs are logged.
  • Outputs are applied intentionally.
  • No vendor classes in the subsystem.
  • Simulation or no-op behavior is present.
  • Commands declare requirements.
  • Disabled behavior is safe.
  • Hardware faults create useful alerts.
  • Documentation and controls pages are updated if behavior is operator-visible.

docs.title: Cookbook: Implement Vision Detection
docs.text: Use this cookbook when adding or changing AprilTag cameras, object detection cameras, Northstar topics, or robot-side vision filtering.

docs.title: 1. Identify the Vision Goal
docs.text:
Decide what the change is meant to provide:

  • Global pose estimation from AprilTags.
  • Fuel detection for autonomous pathing or visualization.
  • Opponent robot detection.
  • Driver or pit diagnostics.
  • Recording or dataset collection.

The goal determines the required camera pose accuracy, frame rate, exposure, logging, and filtering.

docs.title: 2. Add or Update CameraConfig
docs.text:
Edit VisionConstants.cameras. Each CameraConfig needs:

  • poseFunction: robot-to-camera pose as a function of timestamp.
  • id: camera serial or stable identifier published to Northstar.
  • width and height.
  • Exposure, gain, denoise, and color balance.
  • stdDevFactor.
  • fovRads.

Static camera example:

CameraConfig.builder()
    .poseFunction(
        timestamp ->
            Optional.of(
                new Pose3d(
                    xMeters,
                    yMeters,
                    zMeters,
                    new Rotation3d(roll, pitch, yaw))))
    .id("camera-id")
    .width(1280)
    .height(960)
    .exposure(4500)
    .gain(5.0)
    .stdDevFactor(1.0)
    .fovRads(Units.degreesToRadians(90.0))
    .build();

Moving camera example:

.poseFunction(
    timestamp -> {
      var angle = RobotState.getInstance().getSlamAngle(timestamp);
      if (angle.isEmpty()) {
        return Optional.empty();
      }
      return Optional.of(calculateRobotToCamera(angle.get()));
    })

If the camera moves, make sure the relevant mechanism writes timestamped state into RobotState.

docs.title: 3. Configure Northstar Topics
docs.text:
VisionIONorthstar publishes configuration under:

northstar_<index>/config

It subscribes under:

northstar_<index>/output

Make sure the coprocessor publishes:

  • observations for AprilTags.
  • objdetect_observations for object detection.
  • fps_apriltags.
  • fps_objdetect.

The robot publishes timestamp, match info, recording state, layout string, and throttle request. Northstar should consume those fields.

docs.title: 4. Validate AprilTag Pose Estimation
docs.text:
In AdvantageScope, inspect:

  • Vision/Inst*.
  • Vision/AprilTags/Inst*.
  • AprilTagVision/Inst*/RobotPose.
  • AprilTagVision/Inst*/TagPoses.
  • AprilTagVision/RobotPoses.
  • RobotState/EstimatedPose.

Check:

  • Camera pose is on the correct side of the robot.
  • Yaw/pitch/roll signs match WPILib coordinates.
  • Estimated robot pose moves smoothly.
  • Rejected frames are explainable.
  • Latency is plausible.
  • Multi-tag estimates have lower uncertainty than long-distance single-tag estimates.

docs.title: 5. Tune Filtering
docs.text:
Filtering lives mostly in VisionConstants and Vision.periodic().

Adjust carefully:

  • ambiguityThreshold for one-tag pose ambiguity.
  • fieldBorderMargin for off-field rejection.
  • zMin and zMax for impossible robot height.
  • xyStdDevCoefficient and thetaStdDevCoefficient for pose estimator trust.
  • Per-camera stdDevFactor for camera-specific trust.

Do not tune by feel alone. Compare logs before and after.

docs.title: 6. Add Object Detection Handling
docs.text:
Object detection frames are processed in chunks of 10 values:

class, confidence, tx0, ty0, tx1, ty1, tx2, ty2, tx3, ty3

The existing robot-side classes are:

  • 0: fuel.
  • 1: robot.

For a new class, add a new case in Vision.periodic() and define a new observation record or handler in ObjectDetection if needed.

docs.title: 7. Project tx/ty to Field Coordinates
docs.text:
ObjectDetection uses:

  • Timestamped robot pose from RobotState.
  • Timestamped robot-to-camera pose from VisionConstants.
  • Camera roll compensation.
  • Camera pitch and target height.
  • Field bounds rejection.
  • Spatial deduplication.

When adding a target type, define:

  • Which bounding-box points represent the ground contact or physical reference.
  • The expected target height.
  • Maximum persistence time.
  • Deduplication distance.
  • Whether observations should be accepted in teleop, autonomous, sim, or real mode.

docs.title: 8. Add Diagnostics
docs.text:
Log enough to debug calibration and filtering:

  • Raw frame arrays through VisionIO inputs.
  • Accepted object translations.
  • Rejected target counts if needed.
  • Camera FPS.
  • Camera latency.
  • Per-camera robot pose.
  • Selected AprilTag layout.

For a new camera, also document physical mounting and port assignment.

docs.title: 9. Test Sequence
docs.text:
1. Verify Northstar connects to NetworkTables.
  2. Verify each camera publishes FPS.
  3. Verify camera config topics match the expected IDs and exposure values.
  4. Point at known AprilTags and inspect robot pose.
  5. Move the robot and confirm pose changes in the correct direction.
  6. Test object detection with known field positions.
  7. Replay logs after any filtering change.
  8. Confirm disconnected-camera alerts behave correctly.

docs.title: 10. Documentation Updates
docs.text:
Update these docs when vision changes:

  • 204 Vision & Object Detection for architecture or frame format changes.
  • 402 Hardware Reference Notes for camera IDs, ports, or mounting assumptions.
  • 103 Controls & Operations if drivers get new dashboard controls or warnings.
  • The relevant code comments or constants if a calibration value needs context.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: This glossary defines project terms, FRC terms, and logging vocabulary used throughout the documentation.

docs.title: Robot Code Terms
docs.text: Term Meaning Robot The WPILib lifecycle class. It configures logging, runs the scheduler, handles mode transitions, and records global telemetry. RobotContainer The composition root. It creates subsystems, controllers, autonomous choices, default commands, and button bindings. Subsystem A stateful mechanism or service such as drive, launcher, slamtake, hopper, kicker, vision, LEDs, or trash compactor. Command A schedulable behavior that uses one or more subsystems. Commands should be non-blocking and declare requirements. Requirement The subsystem ownership declared by a command. WPILib uses requirements to interrupt conflicting commands safely. Default command The command that runs on a subsystem when no other command requiring that subsystem is scheduled. FullSubsystem Project base class for normal subsystems that need periodic input processing and after-scheduler output application. VirtualSubsystem Project base class for services that run periodically but are not command requirements, such as vision-style services. Goal Desired high-level mechanism state, such as stop, intake, launch, home, or hold position.

docs.title: AdvantageKit and Logging
docs.text: Term Meaning AdvantageKit Logging and replay framework used to record inputs, outputs, metadata, and replayable robot behavior. AdvantageScope Visualization tool used to inspect WPILOG files, NT4 data, field poses, mechanisms, and time-series values. WPILOG Log file format used for recorded robot and replay data. NT4 NetworkTables 4, used for live robot telemetry and dashboard communication. @AutoLog Annotation used on input classes so AdvantageKit can generate loggable input wrappers. Logger.processInputs Logs measured input objects from IO implementations. Logger.recordOutput Logs derived values, setpoints, visualization data, command state, and diagnostics. @AutoLogOutput Annotation for values computed from object state and logged automatically. Replay Desktop workflow where logged inputs from a WPILOG drive robot logic and produce a new _sim log. Determinism Property that replaying the same inputs through the same logic should produce the same decisions and outputs. LoggedTracer Project utility for recording loop timing checkpoints.

docs.title: Hardware Abstraction
docs.text: Term Meaning IO interface Boundary between subsystem logic and hardware or simulator details. Defines logged inputs and requested outputs. Inputs Measured facts such as position, velocity, voltage, current, temperature, connection status, or camera frames. Outputs Requested actions such as voltage, brake/coast, position setpoint, velocity setpoint, or feedforward. No-op IO Implementation that does nothing and reports safe defaults. Used when real hardware is unavailable or during replay paths. Real IO Implementation that talks to vendor hardware APIs and configures devices. Sim IO Implementation that uses physics models or simulated sensors while preserving the same subsystem logic. CAN ID Numeric identifier for a CAN device such as a motor controller, gyro, or encoder. CAN bus Physical or logical CAN network. This project documents bus names where they matter. Current limit Motor controller configuration that limits electrical current to reduce brownout and hardware damage risk.

docs.title: Robot Modes
docs.text: Term Meaning DARWIN Main 2026 robot type. This should be the default merge-safe robot selection. ALPHABOT Secondary/test robot type with separate constants where needed. SIMBOT Desktop physics simulation robot type selected by setting the SIMBOT environment variable. REAL Mode used on the RoboRIO for real robot execution. SIM Mode used for desktop physics simulation. REPLAY Mode used for desktop log replay when running Darwin or Alphabot off-robot. tuningMode Constants flag that enables dashboard-backed tunables. It should be false before merging.

docs.title: Vision and Field Terms
docs.text: Term Meaning Northstar Coprocessor-side vision stack that publishes AprilTag and object detection frames to NetworkTables. AprilTag layout JSON field layout describing where AprilTags are located for pose estimation. Camera transform Robot-relative pose of a camera. Moving cameras need timestamped mechanism state to compute this correctly. Timestamped observation Sensor observation associated with the time it was captured, not the time robot code received it. Standard deviation Measurement uncertainty passed into pose estimation. Larger values make observations less trusted. Object detection Vision output identifying objects such as fuel or robots by class ID and image-space corners. tx / ty Horizontal and vertical angular offsets from camera center to a detected target point. Fuel Game piece tracked by object detection and simulation. Hub shift Project utility for handling shifted hub timing/state information.

docs.title: Controls and Operations
docs.text: Term Meaning Primary controller Driver controller on port 0. Secondary controller Operator controller on port 1. Override switches Physical switch panel on port 5 used for robot-relative drive, coast, and launch-related overrides. Field-relative drive Joystick translation is interpreted relative to the field. Robot-relative drive Joystick translation is interpreted relative to the robot's current heading. Coast override Disabled-mode override that allows selected mechanisms to coast when safe. Dashboard chooser Driver Station or AdvantageKit-backed chooser used for auto routines, AprilTag layouts, or questions.

docs.title: Autonomous and Trajectories
docs.text: Term Meaning Auto routine Complete autonomous command sequence selected from the dashboard. Auto question Dashboard-configurable response that changes how an auto routine is built. Choreo Trajectory design/generation tool used for pathing assets. VTS Project trajectory generation/deploy format used under src/main/deploy/vts. Trajectory reset Pose reset to a trajectory's initial sample. It must be intentional because a bad reset can hide localization issues. Alliance flipping Mirroring or transforming field coordinates so the same logic works from either alliance side.
